story:
Justin, Jake, and Elan headed northeast toward the mountains, hoping to reach the great inland sea whose northern waters fed the river to the Valley of the Black Dog, their home. As they climbed out of dense forest into foothills, the landscape thinned: giant redwoods gave way to smaller pines and scrub oak; grasses and shrubs spread over steeper, stonier ground; streams narrowed and ran sharper. Days of wind-driven rain had soaked them through their blankets. The air cooled, the days shortened, and their spirits sagged, yet they pressed on, up one hill and down another, always gaining height.

Higher on the mountain, trees became scarce and the trail rocky. Behind them lay a spectacular sweep of forest and coastal range; before them loomed the rugged mountain, daunting and gray. From above, they watched clouds pour over the western range and tumble down in wisps that carried rain. They scrambled over slick rock and crossed swift streams until they reached the ridge Justin had spotted. There, at last, they saw it: a vast blue-green sheet of water stretching north, lit by a sudden, blessingly bright sun. Their energy returned. They decided to follow the ridge and look for a safe descent.

After hours, the ridge narrowed into a ledge high above the valley. They walked single file, dizzy at the height, thrilled by the view. The ledge hugged the mountain, weaving with its contours, until Justin turned a corner and stopped. Not twenty feet ahead stood a man. Surprised, both parties stared. The man seemed annoyed.

“Hello,” Justin called. “Nice day.”

“Is it? You’re blocking my way,” the man replied. The ledge was too narrow to pass. No one wanted to turn back the distance they had come. The man sat down.

The boys whispered. Jake wanted action; Elan counseled patience. Together they chose to wait. Instead of stewing, they took in the world spread beneath them. Hours later Justin tried again. “Yo, mister!” No answer. The man had slipped his hat over his eyes and fallen asleep. They dared not creep past for fear of startling him into a fatal flinch.

At last he stirred. “You woke me up. What do you want?”

“To pass,” Justin said. “We’re headed to the lake and, someday, the Valley of the Black Dog. Have you heard of it?”

“I have. It’s far, that way,” the man said, pointing north. He hadn’t been there. He explained he had chosen south—west was blocked by mountain, east was a sheer drop, and winter made the north uninviting. Elan invited him along. The valley, he said, was sheltered; ocean-warmed air kept winters mild. The company would be good.

“Okay,” the man said at last. “I’ll change direction.” He turned and began to walk north. “You can call me Igashu,” he added—“the wanderer.” The boys followed.

Justin smiled at Elan. Their patience had been just enough. No risky scrambling, no weary retreat—only a pause, a view, and a new companion. As they caught up to Igashu, they fell into step together, swapping stories of where they had been, and letting the narrow path lead them forward.
